question.text: What is the theoretical relationship between USDINR call option premium and USDINR put option premium with the same strike and expiry?

answer.text: Put-call parity for currency options: C - P = S × e^(-rf×T) - K × e^(-rd×T), where rf is USD rate and rd is INR rate
C = P always for the same strike and expiry
C - P = S - K at all times
There is no relationship between USDINR call and put premiums

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Currency put-call parity: C - P = S × e^(-rf×T) - K × e^(-rd×T). For equity: C - P = S - K × e^(-r×T). The key difference is that currency parity discounts BOTH the spot (by foreign rate) and the strike (by domestic rate), reflecting that both currencies earn interest. **The Trap:** C ≠ P (they're only equal when ATM forward). C - P ≠ S - K (that ignores discounting). There IS a mathematical relationship. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I Ch4 covers currency put-call parity. Compare with equity put-call parity in Series VIII Ch4. This is a key Series XIII comparison. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put-call parity violations in currency options signal arbitrage opportunities — conversion/reversal strategies can exploit these efficiently on liquid exchange-traded options.
